title: CQRS & Read Models
summary: Command Query Responsibility Segregation, building optimized read models, projections, and handling eventual consistency.

sections.content:
## CQRS in Brief

CQRS separates the model used for **writes (Commands)** from the model used for **reads (Queries)**. This acknowledges that the data model optimized for writes (normalized, enforces invariants) is rarely optimal for reads (denormalized, join-free).

```
Commands → Write Model (domain model, normalized, consistent)
Queries  → Read Model (denormalized, indexed for specific queries)
```

sections.title: CQRS Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    Client --> CmdBus[Command Bus]
    Client --> QueryBus[Query Bus]
    CmdBus --> Handler[Command Handler]
    Handler --> Domain[Domain Model]
    Domain --> WriteDB[(Write DB\nNormalized)]
    Domain --> Events[Domain Events]
    Events --> Projector[Projection Handler]
    Projector --> ReadDB1[(Read DB 1\nOrder Summary)]
    Projector --> ReadDB2[(Read DB 2\nSearch Index)]
    Projector --> ReadDB3[(Read DB 3\nDashboard Stats)]
    QueryBus --> QueryHandler[Query Handler]
    QueryHandler --> ReadDB1
    style Domain fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Projector fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.title: Eventual Consistency in CQRS
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant CS as Command Side
    participant ES as Event Stream
    participant RS as Read Side
    C->>CS: PlaceOrder command
    CS->>CS: Domain logic, save
    CS->>ES: OrderPlaced event
    CS-->>C: 202 Accepted {orderId}
    Note over ES,RS: Async projection update
    ES->>RS: OrderPlaced event processed
    RS->>RS: Update OrderSummary table
    C->>RS: GET /orders/{id}
    RS-->>C: May return stale data briefly
    Note over C: Client must handle eventual consistency

sections.title: CQRS ≠ Event Sourcing
sections.content: CQRS and Event Sourcing are complementary but independent. You can have CQRS with a traditional write store (not event-sourced) — just separate your read and write models. Event sourcing naturally works well with CQRS because events drive projections, but CQRS alone is a simpler step you can take first.

sections.question: Why might you want multiple different read models from the same domain events?
options: To reduce the number of domain events needed
Different query patterns need different data structures — a list view needs different data than a detail view or search index
To avoid eventual consistency in the main read model
Multiple read models allow for transactions across queries
sections.explanation: A list view needs minimal denormalized data for fast rendering. A detail page needs complete nested data. A search feature needs a full-text index. An analytics dashboard needs aggregates. Each of these is optimally served by a different data structure. CQRS lets you build each read model independently from the same event stream.
